[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: dog
- fur color: light brown/tan
- fur texture: shaggy/fluffy
- eyes: visible, dark
- nose: visible, dark
- mouth: open, tongue visible
- posture: lying down
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light brown/tan AND fur texture = shaggy/fluffy] THEN [35]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND eyes = orange] THEN [9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND posture = sitting upright] THEN [27]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 35 fires because the Query Image shows an animal that is clearly a dog, with light brown/tan, shaggy/fluffy fur, which matches the observable features of the example for class 35.
</rule_check>
<response>35</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

What is the class of this image?

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [35, 9, 27]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: light grey/tan
- eye color: amber/yellowish-brown
- face shape: round
- ears: pointed, medium size
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light brown/tan AND eyes are closed or partially closed] THEN [35]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = light grey/tan AND eye color = amber/yellowish-brown AND face shape = round] THEN [9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = dark grey AND eye color = green AND body is slender] THEN [27]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 9 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with light grey/tan fur, amber/yellowish-brown eyes, and a round face, which matches the observable features for this class.
</rule_check>
<response>9</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: grey
- tail: long and thin
- body posture: crouching/walking on a wall
- eyes: light colored
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light brown AND body posture = lying down] THEN [35]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = light grey AND body posture = sitting with front paws forward AND eyes = orange] THEN [9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND body posture = sitting upright AND tail = long and thin] THEN [27]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 27 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with grey fur, a long and thin tail, and a body posture that is consistent with the general characteristics of a cat, even if not strictly "sitting upright" as in the example, the overall appearance aligns more with 27 than 9 or 35. The cat in the query image is also a Russian Blue, which is the breed associated with class 27.
</rule_check>
<response>27</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

Valid labels: [35, 9, 27]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.
